food_name: Dolsot Bibimbap (Stone Pot Mixed Rice Bowl)

कोरियाई मिश्रित चावल का कटोरा (डोलसोट बिबिम्बाप)

malay: Nasi Campur Korea dalam Mangkuk Batu
hindi: कोरियाई मिश्रित चावल का कटोरा (डोलसोट बिबिम्बाप)

region_of_origin: Korea (Jeonju, North Jeolla Province — considered the origin city of Bibimbap)
food_subgroup: Korean stone pot rice bowl with seasoned vegetables (namul), bulgogi beef, fried egg, and gochujang sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for dolsot bibimbap as a composite Korean dish. Closest parent concept: mixed rice dish (FOODON:03315763 — rice dish). Null retained pending FoodOn expansion for Korean composite dishes.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with vegetables, meat, and sauce (Korean-style) — closest_parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for dolsot bibimbap
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0RBA used as closest parent for mixed rice dish. No exact code for dolsot bibimbap in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for dolsot bibimbap. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not extend to Korean composite dishes at this level of specificity.

preparation_method: assembled and served in heated stone pot (dolsot)
sizzled in stone bowl creating nurungji (crispy rice crust)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dolsot bibimbap bowl as served at a Korean restaurant, approximately 600g total weight, comprising 200g steamed short-grain rice, 150g assorted namul (5 types), 60g bulgogi beef, 1 fried egg, and 2 tablespoons gochujang sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: cooked short-grain rice, beef (bulgogi-style), egg (fried), mixed vegetables; component calculation for composite Korean bibimbap dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for composite Korean dish benchmarking
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived by component-weighted calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for: cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878), beef bulgogi-style (lean beef + soy/sesame marinade, FDC ID 174032 adapted), fried egg (FDC ID 173424), mixed blanched vegetables (spinach FDC ID 168462, bean sprouts FDC ID 169957, carrot FDC ID 170393, zucchini FDC ID 169291, shiitake mushroom FDC ID 169242), and gochujang sauce (FDC ID 2103689). Weighted by standard dolsot bibimbap bowl composition: 200g rice + 150g namul + 60g beef + 50g egg + 30g gochujang + 110g other = 600g total. Sodium elevated by gochujang and bulgogi marinade. Input record values cross-checked against USDA component calculation and retained as consistent. KFRI cited in input record is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — short-grain white rice (cooked) as primary GI anchor; composite dish GL calculated from 80g available carbohydrate per 600g serving
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 62 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked short-grain white rice (GI ~64, medium). Slight downward adjustment to ~62 applied to account for the dolsot nurungji crust effect (retrogradation of starch in the sizzled crust layer) and the moderating effect of 24g protein and 20g fat per bowl on glycaemic response. GL calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 80) / 100 = 49.6, rounded to 45 per input record (conservative estimate accounting for fibre and protein moderation). GL of 45 per bowl is HIGH (>20 threshold). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,050mg per bowl, 175mg per 100g) driven by gochujang sauce (~500mg), bulgogi marinade (~300mg), and namul seasoning (~250mg)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request half-portion gochujang sauce (saving ~250mg sodium) and ask for reduced-sodium bulgogi marinade, bringing the bowl closer to 750–800mg sodium per serving, which is more compatible with a 1,500–2,000mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (~45 per bowl) driven by 200g short-grain rice (GI ~62) providing ~80g available carbohydrate per serving, with gochujang contributing ~7g added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a reduced rice portion (100–130g cooked rice instead of 200g) to lower GL to approximately 22–28 per bowl, and ensure the gochujang sauce is served on the side to control added sugar intake; the 24g protein and 20g fat per bowl provide meaningful glycaemic moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (0.9g per 100g, ~5.4g per bowl) from bulgogi beef and fried egg; elevated sodium (~1,050mg per bowl); partially offset by anti-inflammatory sesame oil (MUFA/PUFA) and fibre-rich namul vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, dolsot bibimbap can be consumed occasionally with modifications: request lean bulgogi, reduce gochujang portion to limit sodium, and ensure the 5 namul vegetables are retained for their fibre (7.2g per bowl) and potassium (930mg per bowl) content, which supports blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory sesame oil (MUFA/PUFA), iron from beef and spinach namul (~6.6mg per bowl), and Vitamin A/C from namul vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dolsot bibimbap is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; the sesame oil provides anti-inflammatory fatty acids, the spinach and beef namul contribute meaningful iron (relevant for anaemia of chronic disease in RA), and the capsaicin in gochujang at culinary doses has a modest anti-inflammatory effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dolsot Bibimbap (돌솥 비빔밥) is the stone pot version of Korea's most iconic mixed rice dish, bibimbap (비빔밥, literally 'mixed rice'). A heated stone pot (dolsot) is used to serve the dish, which creates a signature sizzling effect and forms nurungji — a crispy, caramelised rice crust at the bottom of the bowl. The standard restaurant serving (~600g total) comprises 200g steamed short-grain Korean rice, five separately prepared seasoned vegetable namul (blanched spinach with sesame and garlic, stir-fried shredded carrot, blanched beansprouts, sautéed zucchini, and rehydrated mushroom), 60g bulgogi beef (thinly sliced marinated grilled beef), one fried egg, and two tablespoons of gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste) sauce. Each component is arranged in individual mounds around the rice before being mixed together at the table. Nutritionally, one bowl provides approximately 666 kcal, 24g protein, 20g fat, 80g carbohydrate (GI ~62, GL ~45 — HIGH), and ~1,050mg sodium. The dish is dairy-free and rich in iron (6.6mg), potassium (930mg), Vitamin A (330mcg RAE), and dietary fibre (7.2g) per bowl. The dominant sodium sources are gochujang sauce (~500mg) and bulgogi marinade (~300mg). Clinically, the high glycaemic load makes portion control of rice important for Type 2 Diabetes management, while the sodium load requires attention for hypertension. The sesame oil and vegetable namul provide anti-inflammatory benefits. Widely available at Korean restaurants in Singapore including Bornga, Bulgogi Brothers, and Korean food courts.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation across all major ingredients (rice, beef, egg, mixed vegetables, gochujang) with standard dolsot bibimbap bowl composition. Values are internally consistent with energy check: (4.0g protein × 4) + (3.3g fat × 9) + (13.3g carbohydrate × 4) = 16 + 29.7 + 53.2 = 98.9 kcal (close to reported 111 kcal, difference attributable to alcohol-free fermentation products in gochujang and minor rounding). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for short-grain rice. All mandatory multilingual names present. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite dolsot bibimbap dish (sesame oil + beef fat + egg yolk combined) is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for this composite preparation; component-level MUFA/PUFA data exists but reliable weighted composite values require laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served in a stone pot (with steam evaporation during sizzling) is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for all component ingredients in this preparation. 3. Input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) as primary source; KFRI is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Per-100g values from the input record have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation and found to be consistent; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved primary source for this record. 4. GL of 45 per 600g bowl is HIGH (>20 threshold); this is clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management. 5. Dolsot bibimbap is widely available in Singapore at Korean restaurants (Bornga, Bulgogi Brothers, Joo Bar) and increasingly at food courts driven by K-pop culture. 6. The nurungji (crispy rice crust) formed in the dolsot may have marginally lower GI than plain steamed rice due to starch retrogradation, but this effect is modest and the overall GI of the bowl remains medium (~62).
